# Inference Efficiency {.section-divider}

## Why Speed Matters in Robot Control

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
- Control loops have strict timing budgets.
- Slow inference increases reaction delay.
- Contact-rich tasks may require fast corrections.
- Worst-case latency matters more than nice average timing.
:::

::: {}
![](assets/figures/control_loop_latency.svg){width=100%}
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Main point: latency is a control issue, not only a software performance metric.

If a robot reacts late, the physical situation may already have changed. This is especially true around contact, where small delays can create large force errors or make the robot continue pushing after it should have adapted.

For diffusion policies, the denoising loop is often the dominant cost. But the total query time also includes sensing, observation encoding, communication, synchronization, and low-level execution.
:::

---

## Denoising Steps vs Latency

Let $K$ be the number of denoising steps and $T_{net}$ the cost of one denoising network evaluation:

::: {.equation-card}
$$
T_{infer}
\approx
K \cdot T_{net}
$$
:::

For a policy query, a more realistic budget is:

$$
T_{query}
=
T_{sense}
+
T_{enc}
+
K T_{net}
+
T_{comm}
$$

::: {.notes}
Main point: the number of denoising steps multiplies the neural network cost.

This simple equation is useful for intuition. If one denoising pass takes 4 ms, then 50 steps already cost about 200 ms before sensing and communication. That may be acceptable for slow tabletop behavior, but not for fast contact correction.

The approximation hides implementation details. GPU synchronization, batch size, camera pipelines, and CPU-GPU transfer can dominate in practice. Still, $K$ is the first design knob to inspect.
:::

---

## DDPM Sampling Cost

DDPM sampling uses many small stochastic reverse steps:

::: {.equation-card}
$$
x_{k-1}
=
\mu_\theta(x_k,k)
+
\sigma_k z,
\qquad
z \sim \mathcal{N}(0,I)
$$
:::

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
Benefits:

- faithful to the original probabilistic sampler
- smooth incremental refinement

Costs:

- many network evaluations
- stochasticity can add rollout variability
- hard to fit into fast control loops
:::

::: {.warning}
A sampler that is excellent for offline generation can be too slow for online control.
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Main point: DDPM is the baseline sampler but is often expensive.

The stochastic term is part of the reverse process. In robot action space, stochastic sampling is not automatically bad, because multimodality can be useful. But uncontrolled variability and long inference time are both deployment concerns.

For a lecture, DDPM is the clean starting point. For a robot, we usually ask how many steps are really necessary.
:::

---

## DDIM for Fewer Steps

DDIM uses a non-Markovian reverse trajectory and can skip diffusion timesteps:

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
- same training objective as DDPM-style noise prediction
- fewer denoising evaluations at inference
- deterministic when the stochasticity parameter is zero
- useful for latency-constrained policy queries
:::

::: {}
![](assets/figures/ddpm_ddim_comparison.svg){width=100%}
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Main point: DDIM is a practical acceleration path.

The key idea is that we do not necessarily need to traverse every reverse step. DDIM defines a trajectory through a subset of timesteps. With zero stochasticity, the sample is determined by the initial noise and network predictions.

In the PushT notebook, the contrast is educational: a DDPM sampler may use around 100 steps, while a DDIM sampler can demonstrate a much smaller number such as 10 steps. The exact number is a design and validation choice, not a universal constant.
:::

---

## Quality, Latency, and Stability

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
Reducing $K$ improves latency, but can change behavior:

- less refinement of the action chunk
- possible lower task success
- possible rougher trajectories
- different closed-loop stability properties

The right sampler is task- and hardware-dependent.
:::

::: {}
![](assets/figures/sampling_steps_latency.svg){width=100%}
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Main point: fewer steps are not automatically better.

For image generation, we may judge the output visually. For robot policies, we must test closed-loop behavior. A visually smooth action chunk can still be unsafe or ineffective if it reacts too late, violates force constraints, or creates unstable contact.

The design target is not maximum generative quality in isolation. The target is enough action quality inside the timing and safety constraints of the robot.
:::

---

## Action Chunking and Replanning Frequency

Action chunking amortizes inference cost:

::: {.equation-card}
$$
\text{query once}
\rightarrow
\text{execute } h \text{ actions}
\rightarrow
\text{query again}
$$
:::

Tradeoff:

- larger $h$: lower query frequency, more stale actions
- smaller $h$: more reactive, higher inference burden
- larger $H$: more temporal context, harder sequence modeling

::: {.notes}
Main point: inference efficiency is also controlled by execution horizon and replanning frequency.

If sampling is expensive, we can execute more actions per query. This amortizes inference cost, but it also means the robot follows an older plan for longer. In contact, this can be risky.

The prediction horizon $H$ and execution horizon $h$ should be chosen together with the sampler. They are part of the control design, not only the machine learning architecture.
:::

---

## Model-Side Acceleration

Sampler changes are only one option. Other directions include:

- smaller 1D U-Net backbones
- lower-dimensional action parameterizations
- policy distillation into a feed-forward policy
- consistency-style or few-step diffusion samplers
- hardware-aware deployment and compilation

::: {.takeaway}
Acceleration is useful only if the closed-loop robot behavior remains stable, reactive, and task competent.
:::

::: {.notes}
Main point: give a broader menu of acceleration strategies.

Reducing the number of denoising steps is the most direct approach, but it is not the only one. We can also simplify the network, reduce the action representation, distill a multi-step sampler into a faster policy, or optimize deployment.

The evaluation criterion must remain closed-loop robot behavior. Offline denoising loss and sample appearance are not enough.
:::

---

## Slow-Fast Policy Interface

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
A useful robotics pattern:

- a slower diffusion policy proposes coherent action chunks
- a faster low-level controller tracks or filters commands
- compliance control reacts quickly to measured interaction forces
:::

::: {.equation-card}
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{diffusion policy}
&\rightarrow
\text{reference motion} \\
&\rightarrow
\text{compliant controller}
\end{aligned}
$$
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Main point: transition from inference efficiency to compliant control.

If the diffusion policy cannot run at the same rate as the low-level control loop, we should not force it to be the only control mechanism. A more realistic architecture is hierarchical: the diffusion policy proposes a motion or action chunk, and a faster compliant controller handles immediate physical interaction.

This motivates the next section. For compliant robots, the action may be more than a target position. It can also include stiffness, damping, or admittance parameters that determine how the robot physically responds to contact.
:::
